coredata.dc:title: Preparation of a composite material based on self-assembly of biomass carbon dots and sodium alginate hydrogel and its green, efficient and visual adsorption performance for Pb2+
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Siyi
Arkin, Kamile
Zheng, Yuxin
Ma, Jicheng
Bei, Yuyang
Liu, Di
Shang, Qingkun
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, an environmentally friendly, visible and easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent (SA@PEI-CDs, SA: sodium alginate, PEI: polyethyleneimine, CDs: carbon dots) has been prepared from functionalized positively charged biomass carbons dots (PEI-CDs) and anionic polyelectrolyte sodium alginate (SA) through electrostatic self-assembly. SA@PEI-CDs can efficiently and preferentially adsorb Pb2+. Compared to bare SA, the adsorption capacity of SA@PEI-CDs increased from 206.93 mg/g to 380.39 mg/g (at pH=4), which enhanced 83.82%. It can still maintain 85.67% of the original adsorption capacity after five cycles. The quenching of the blue fluorescence of the adsorbent derived from carbon dots indicates that the adsorption process has reached saturation. The investigation about the adsorption isotherm models, kinetics and thermodynamic parameters reveals that the adsorption process follows Freundlich isotherm model based on chemical adsorption, which is an endothermic spontaneous reaction with an increase in entropy. The possible adsorption mechanism is electrostatic attraction, ion exchange, and together with the coordination of -NH2, -OH, -COOH with Pb2+. In addition, the extremely easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent is also used in the multi-layer adsorption and separation of simulated sewage samples in the water purifier model. For simulated water samples whose concentration of five/three types of heavy metal ions exceeds 100 times the standard, after using adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs for two or three consecutive adsorptions, the content of heavy metals decreases rapidly, which is far below the limit value of high-quality water. The Pb2+ ion concentration decreased from 1.0 mg/L to 0.002540 mg/L and 0.003611 mg/L, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Biomass carbon dots
Sodium alginate hydrogel
Pb2+
Visible and easy-to-recycle

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent sodium alginate hydrogel (SA@PEI-CDs)
The effect of PEI on the properties of CDs
The adsorption performance of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb 2+
The absorption performance of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb2+
Adsorption of simulated samples of surface water and drinking water

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22172021, 21872022, 21573039 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CDs (A), PEI-CDs (B) (The upper right inset is the HR-TEM image, the lower left inset is the particle size distribution). FI-IR spectra (C), XPS spectra (D), UV–vis absorption spectra (E) and normalized fluorescence emission spectra (F) of CDs and PEI-CDs.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs (A) and PEI-CDs (B) under different excitation wavelengths; fluorescence decay curve of CDs (C) and PEI-CDs (D); the response of CDs (E) and PEI-CDs (F) towards metal ions.
Fluorescence intensity of CDs (A) and PEI-CDs (B) at different concentrations of NaCl; Fluorescence intensity of CDs (C) and PEI-CDs (D) at different pH from 1 to 13.
Photos of SA beads under sunlight (A) and UV-light (B), fresh SA@PEI-CDs under sunlight (C) and UV-light (D), SA@PEI-CDs adsorption Pb2+ 3 h (E) and 6 h (F).
The influence of pH (A), dose (B) and the adsorption curve on different initial Pb2+concentration (C) and time (D).

Competitive adsorption of Pb2+ coexisting with some metal ions (A), the regeneration performance (B) and reuse stability of SA@PEI-CD for 5 cycles (C). (In Fig. 6(A)) the pink line represents Pb2+, the blue line represents coexisting ions).
Fitted curves of Langmuir adsorption isotherm model (A), Freundlich adsorption isotherm model (B), PFO model(C) and PSO of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb2+ adsorption.
Thermogravimetry curves (A), FI-IR spectra (B) and UV–vis spectrum (C) of SA, SA@PEI-CDs and SA@PEI-CDs+Pb2+, Intra-particle diffusion model of SA@PEI-CDs@ for Pb2+ adsorption (D).
XPS survey of SA, SA@PEI-CDs and SA@PEI-CDs+Pb2+ (A) and the high-resolution Pb4f (B), Ca2p (C), C1s (D), N1s (E) and O1s (F).
Comparison of Pb2+ adsorption capacity based on various types of adsorbents.

row.entry: Fe/Co metal organic complex nanosheet modified by 1,4-dicarboxybenzene (BDC) (FeCo@BDC)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamics parameters for Pb2+ adsorption of SA@PEI-CDs.

head.ce:title: Preparation of a composite material based on self-assembly of biomass carbon dots and sodium alginate hydrogel and its green, efficient and visual adsorption performance for Pb2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Northeast Normal University, 130024 Changchun, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Northeast Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Nanobiosensing and Nanobioanalysis at Universities of Jilin Province, Northeast Normal University, 130024, Changchun, P.R.China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, an environmentally friendly, visible and easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent (SA@PEI-CDs, SA: sodium alginate, PEI: polyethyleneimine, CDs: carbon dots) has been prepared from functionalized positively charged biomass carbons dots (PEI-CDs) and anionic polyelectrolyte sodium alginate (SA) through electrostatic self-assembly. SA@PEI-CDs can efficiently and preferentially adsorb Pb2+. Compared to bare SA, the adsorption capacity of SA@PEI-CDs increased from 206.93 mg/g to 380.39 mg/g (at pH=4), which enhanced 83.82%. It can still maintain 85.67% of the original adsorption capacity after five cycles. The quenching of the blue fluorescence of the adsorbent derived from carbon dots indicates that the adsorption process has reached saturation. The investigation about the adsorption isotherm models, kinetics and thermodynamic parameters reveals that the adsorption process follows Freundlich isotherm model based on chemical adsorption, which is an endothermic spontaneous reaction with an increase in entropy. The possible adsorption mechanism is electrostatic attraction, ion exchange, and together with the coordination of -NH2, -OH, -COOH with Pb2+. In addition, the extremely easy-to-recycle spherical adsorbent is also used in the multi-layer adsorption and separation of simulated sewage samples in the water purifier model. For simulated water samples whose concentration of five/three types of heavy metal ions exceeds 100 times the standard, after using adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs for two or three consecutive adsorptions, the content of heavy metals decreases rapidly, which is far below the limit value of high-quality water. The Pb2+ ion concentration decreased from 1.0 mg/L to 0.002540 mg/L and 0.003611 mg/L, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of modern industry, the harm of heavy metal pollution to humans and environment has always been a major issue that needs to be resolved, because heavy metals are difficult to be degraded and easy to accumulate and migrate in the ecosystem [1]. For example, Pb2+ in human body can affect human intelligence and bone development, cause indigestion and endocrine disorders, high blood pressure and arrhythmia, and damage kidney and immune function [2,3]. There are many methods to remove the heavy metal pollution, such as precipitation method [4], electrolysis method [5], redox method [6] and adsorption method [7,8]. Although the adsorption method is one of the effective methods to remove heavy metal pollution, problems such as low adsorption efficiency and difficulty in the recovery and reuse of adsorbents are still the key factors that limit its wide application. Some adsorbents have high adsorption efficiency for heavy metal ions, but their own instability during the adsorption process will also bring new threats to the environment. Therefore, the development of new adsorbents that are environmentally friendly, stable and efficient, easy to recycle and regenerate has become a research hotspot.

Carbon dots (CDs) are well-known fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. They are usually monodispersed spheres with a size of less than 10 nm [9]. The surface and boundary of CDs are rich in nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing active functional groups, making them rich in photoluminescence, chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, easy to functionalization and relatively non-cytotoxicity [10]. Therefore, CDs have been widely used in many fields such as biological imaging [11], catalysis [12], optoelectronic devices [13] and detection [14]. Recently, much attention has been paid to the synthesis of CDs derived from biomass. Biomass is cheap and easy to obtain, and some is even biological waste. They are rich in carbon element and are easily converted into CDs with different surface functional groups and special properties. For example, Zhixiong Hu [15] and co-workers used the green tea leaf residue as the carbon source to produce CDs (named as T-CDs). T-CDs was sensitive to temperature and used as the fluorescence sensor for gefitinib determination. Cheng et al., [16] synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (N/S-CDs) by a facile approach based on one-step combustion treatment of cellulose-based biowaste of willow catkin. Biomass derived CDs usually have abundant functional groups and are therefore easy to combine with metal ions. Therefore, many methods have been developed to detect metal ions using biomass CDs [14,16–18]. These methods have fast detection speed, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. It is very promising to solve the problems of expensive equipment and complicated operation of traditional detection methods. However, most biomass CDs have excellent water solubility, which brings certain difficulties to further separation.

Researchers reported the combination of CDs and hydrogel to prepare new functional polymer materials [19,20]. Hydrogel is a three-dimensional network composed of hydrophilic polymers and a large number of water molecules. It has both solid and liquid properties and can swell but cannot dissolve [21]. The abundant functional groups on the hydrogel can be cross-linked with CDs through covalent or non-covalent bond, which becomes a matrix for accommodating fluorescent CDs [22]. According to the source of hydrogels, there are CDs/small molecule gel composite hydrogels [23], CDs/natural polymer composite hydrogels [24], and CDs/synthetic polymer composite hydrogels [25]. Sodium alginate (SA) is a natural and renewable polysaccharide polymer. It is composed of β-D-mannuronic acid (M) and α-L-guluronic acid (G), which are connected by 1–4 glycosidic bonds to form a linear polymer [26]. SA is derived from alginic acid, which is widely present in the cytoplasm of brown algae. The -COO- functional groups of SA chain are easily chelated with divalent or polyvalent metal ions [27]. SA has excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability and strong affinity, and has been used in medical, food and environmental fields [28]. There are some reports describing the adsorption characteristics of SA hydrogel for different heavy metal ions [29,30], but the adsorption capacity is relatively poor. To improve the adsorption performance of SA, the numbers and types of functional groups that can chelate with heavy metal ions should be increased.

In this paper, we prepared a new type of CDs using the broccoli stem (the part discarded when eating) as raw material through one-pot hydrothermal method. After functionalizing CDs with polyethyleneimine (PEI), a new type of solid spherical adsorbent (SA@PEI-CDs) was prepared by self-assembly electrostatic interaction with SA hydrogel. The research results show that SA@PEI-CDs can efficiently and preferentially adsorb Pb2+. By investigating the thermodynamic and kinetic characteristics of the adsorption process, the adsorption mechanism of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb2+ is proposed. The raw material source of SA@PEI-CDs is environmentally friendly, and the solid sphere are easy to separate, recover and reuse. The quenching of the blue fluorescence of the adsorbent derived from PEI-CDs indicates that the adsorption process has reached saturation. Furthermore, we also prepared mixed simulated sewage samples according to the national surface water environmental quality standards and drinking water sanitation standards, and discussed the adsorption and purification performance of SA@PEI-CDs on variety of heavy metals using our own multi-layer water purifier model. The results show that SA@PEI-CDs has a good ability to remove multiple heavy metals at the same time. Therefore, SA@PEI-CDs is expected to play an important role in water purification and environmental protection.

Broccoli was purchased from the local supermarket in Changchun. Sodium alginate (SA), Ce(NO3)3, Yb(NO3)3, Gd(NO3)3, Nd(NO3)3, Tm(NO3)3, La(NO3)3 and Er(NO3)3 were purchased from Aladdin (China), poly (ethylenimine) (PEI) was purchased from Macklin (China). Pb2+ standard solution (1000 μg/mL) dissolved in HNO3 (1 mol/L) was purchased from Beijing Century Aoke Biotechnology Co. Ltd. CaCl2, KCl, MgCl2, NH4Cl, ZnCl2, EDTA-2Na, H2SO4, NaOH, HCl, NaCl, CH3COOH, Cd(NO3)2, Bi(NO3)3, NaF, FeCl3, CuCl2, CoCl2, MnCl2, AlCl3, Na3PO4, Na2SO4, Na2SO3, NaNO3, NaI and Na2CO3 were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works.

The morphology of CDs and hydrogel were measured on Electron Microscope (JEM-2100 PLUS) transmission electron microscope (TEM) and Electron Microscope (HITACHI SU8010) scanning electron microscope (SEM). The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) was measured using Nicolet 6700 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on Thermo ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out at nitrogen atmosphere by DTG-60 H. Ultraviolet absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were carried out by Thermo Fisher UV–vis spectrophotometer and F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (GANGDONG SCI.@TECH. CO.LTD), respectively.

We prepared biomass CDs based on the stems of broccoli through one-pot hydrothermal method. First, the stems of broccoli were freeze-dried and ground into powder. 0.5 g obtained powder was dissolved in 15 mL H2O, and then placed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) for hydrothermal reaction at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The unreacted precursors were removed by centrifugation and filtration, and bright blue fluorescent CDs were finally prepared with a quantum yield of 13.24%.

In order to increase the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs and enhance their binding force with sodium alginate hydrogel, we use PEI (polyethyleneimine) as a passivating agent and prepare PEI-CDs by hydrothermal synthesis. 3 mg PEI was dissolved in 15 mL CDs solution and then transferred into the oven. After reacting at 200 °C for 4 h and filtering through 0.22 µm microfiltration membrane to remove large particle impurities, PEI-CDs with a higher quantum yield of 18.34% was obtained.

2 g sodium alginate (SA) powder was dissolved in 100 mL deionized water and stirred vigorously for 3 h. It was dropwise added into 0.1 mol/L CaCl2 solution to form SA hydrogel under magnetic stirring. To ensure the hydrogel completely cross-linked, the obtained SA hydrogel was soaked in CaCl2 solution for 6 h. Finally, washing them 3 times with H2O to remove excess CaCl2 on the surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent sodium alginate hydrogel (SA@PEI-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g SA hydrogel were soaked in 10 mL PEI-CDs solution for 10 h, so that PEI-CDs are fully integrated with SA hydrogel. Then, SA@PEI-CDs were washed with deionized water three times to remove unbound PEI-CDs. Finally, the blue fluorescent SA hydrogel was fabricated.

10 mg SA@PEI-CDs was placed in 10 mL different initial concentrations Pb2+ solution. Place the mixture for 10 h at room temperature. The remaining Pb2+ concentration in the solution was measured by ICP-AES. All of the adsorption experiments were repeated 3 times. The formula
Q =
(
C
0
−
C
e
) × V
m
(1) was used to evaluate adsorption capacity and
R % =
(
C
0
−
C
e
)
C
0
× 100 %
(2) was used to calculate removal efficiency of Pb2+, where Q (mg/g) represents the adsorption capacity of SA@PEI-CDs towards Pb2+, C0 (mg/L) represents the initial concentration of Pb2+ and Ce (mg/L) represents the equal concentration of Pb2+, V refers to the volume of solution and m refers to the mass of SA@PEI-CDs.

10 mg SA@PEI-CDs was soaked in 600 mg/L Pb2+/Fe3+, Pb2+/Zn2+, Pb2+/Ag+, Pb2+/Cu2+, Pb2+/Cr3+, Pb2+/Ni2+, Pb2+/Cd2+and Pb2+/Cr(Ⅵ) mixed solution and placed at room temperature for 10 h. After adsorption process finished, the concentration of the remaining metal ions in the solution was measured by ICP-AES. The competitive adsorption was compared through the removal efficiency of SA@PEI-CDs towards each metal ion.

Adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs after adsorbing Pb2+ was added to 0.05 mol/L EDTA-2Na solution and ultrasonic treated for 1 h at room temperature. Then, it was washed with deionized water to remove excess EDTA-2Na on the surface. The regenerated adsorbent was subjected to the adsorption experiment according to the method of 2.7.

The TEM images in Fig. 1(A) show that CDs based on the stems of broccoli are spherical and the average particle size is about 2.20 nm. It can be seen from the HR-TEM image (inset in Fig. 1(A)) that the lattice space is 0.21 nm (100 crystal plane of graphene), indicating that CDs may have a graphite-like crystal structure. The FT-IR spectra of CDs is shown in Fig. 1(C). There is a broad and strong absorption peak at 3300 cm−1, which is attributed to the stretching vibration of association state -OH. The absorption peak at 1285 cm−1 is the bending vibration of C-O. The absorption peaks at 1688 cm−1and 1599 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 2930 cm−1 and 2877 cm−1 corresponded to saturated C-H stretching vibration, and the peak at 1404 cm−1 may be the bending vibration of -NH2. XPS spectrum in Fig. 1(D) exhibits that the peaks of C1s (285.07 eV), N1s (400.17 eV) and O1s (532.50 eV) all appeared in CDs spectrum. High-resolution XPS spectra are shown in Fig. S1. The C1s spectrum revealed that the C element mainly exist in CC, C-O/C-N and CO/O-CO, while N component are mainly present as C-N and N-H form and O element mainly exist as CO and C-OH form. Fig. 1(E) shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs. The absorption peak of CDs at 207 nm and 275 nm corresponded to the π-π * transition of CC and n-π * transition of CO, respectively. The n-π * transition is a low-intensity shoulder peak, which is commonly found in CDs prepared from biomass waste [31–33]. This is due to the low contents and weak electronic transitions of CO. Fig. 1(F) is the normalized fluorescence spectrum of CDs. The maximum emission wavelength of CDs is 403 nm and the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) is 13.24% (shown in Fig. S2). It is a relatively high QY in CDs directly prepared from biomass [34–36].

Polyethyleneimine (PEI) is a kind of water-soluble polymer containing nitrogen. It can combine with organic compounds containing oxygen or nitrogen functional groups through hydrogen bonds, which is usually used to modify CDs to improve fluorescence quantum yield and can enhance the interaction between CDs and hydrogel.

PEI-CDs are still spherical but the size is about 3.05 nm (Fig. 1(B)), which is larger than that of original CDs. The abundant -NH2 in PEI is grafted on the surface of CDs through hydrothermal reaction. The enhanced interaction among PEI-CDs resulted in slight aggregation. It can be observed from HR-TEM (inset in Fig. 1(B)) that PEI-CDs also have a graphite-like crystal structure as CDs and the lattice space is 0.34 nm (002 crystal plane of graphene). The FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1(C)) of PEI-CDs are almost the same as that of CDs. Differently, PEI-CDs has double peaks at 3238 cm−1 and 3381 cm−1, corresponding to the symmetry and antisymmetric stretching vibration of -NH2. What’s more, the bending vibration of -NH2 of PEI-CDs at 1398 cm−1 is enhanced. The absorption peak of PEI-CDs at 1110 cm−1 is more obvious, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-N. With regard to XPS spectrum in Fig. 1(D), the peaks of C1s, N1s and O1s also existed in PEI-CDs spectrum and the N1s peak of PEI-CDs is more obvious than that of CDs. High-resolution N1s spectrum is shown in Fig. S1(C). Notably, due to the functionalization of PEI, the content of N-H increased from 50% to 55%, which confirmed that -NH2 in PEI was grafted onto the surface of CDs. The content of nitrogen in PEI-CDs increases from 7.885% to 10.162%, which was verified by elemental analysis. Both UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectrum of PEI-CDs (Fig. 1(E) and (F)) generated a red shift compared with original CDs. The QY increased to 18.34% (Fig. S2) and the color under the UV lamp changed from pure blue to blue-green. It may be due to the increase of -NH2 on the surface of CDs after passivation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of PEI on the properties of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Both CDs and PEI-CDs have excitation wavelength dependence (shown in Fig. 2(A) and Fig. 2(B)), which may be due to their abundant surface functional groups and wide range of particle size distribution. The optimal excitation wavelengths for CDs and PEI-CDs are 337 nm and 365 nm, respectively. Fig. 2(C) and Fig. 2(D) are listed the fluorescence decay curve of CDs and PEI-CDs. Compared with CDs, the average fluorescence lifetime of PEI-CDs was increased from 4.15 ns and 7.12 ns. The effects of 22 metal ions and 11 common anions on the fluorescence intensity of CDs and PEI-CDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. 2(E) and Fig. 2(F), Fe3+ and Ag+ can significantly quench the fluorescence of CDs and PEI-CDs, while Pb2+ can also reduce the fluorescence of PEI-CDs. There are a large number of -NH2 groups in the molecular structure of PEI. From Fig. 1(C), it can be seen that the main difference between the infrared spectral characteristics of CDs and PEI-CDs is that the addition of PEI leads to a significant increase in the vibration absorption of -NH2 and C-N. -NH2 can form a complex with Pb2+, so the increase in the number of -NH2 greatly enhances the binding ability of PEI-CDs and Pb2+, which is the main reason why PEI-CDs are more sensitive to Pb2+ [37]. Commonly, fluorescence intensity of CDs and PEI-CDs can’t be affected by anions (shown in Fig. S3).

The stability of CDs and PEI-CDs at different ionic strength and pH is investigated and the results are shown in Fig. 3. When the concentration of NaCl is greater than 0.2 mol/L, the normalized fluorescence intensity of CDs decreases slightly, while that of PEI-CDs is almost unchanged. Since CDs has a large number of acidic or basic functional groups on the surface, the change of pH will have a great impact on its fluorescence intensity. When they existed in the strong acid or alkali situation, they formed cation or anion. And the surface state has changed, causing the quenching of the fluorescence emission center. It is clear that CDs kept stable at pH from 3 to 9, while PEI-CDs was stable at 4–8. -NH2 is a basic group, the acid-base change of the solution directly affects the protonation of -NH2, so the increase in the amount of -NH2 will inevitably be more sensitive to pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The adsorption performance of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb 2+
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, applications about CDs in adsorption are few reported because most CDs are water-soluble and are hard to separate from aqueous solution. It was only used in the determination of metal ions, not the removal of toxic metal ions [14,17,18]. Therefore, we introduced the prepared PEI-CDs into the sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel matrix to prepare spherical fluorescent hydrogel, which can adsorb and remove heavy metal ions. After surface modification, the -NH2 groups on the surface of PEI-CDs increased, which changed the zeta potential from − 60.1 mV (CDs) to + 59.8 mV (Fig. S4). SA is a kind of polyanionic electrolyte and its zeta potential is − 2.7 mV (Fig. S4). SA and PEI-CDs self-assemble into SA@PEI-CDs through electrostatic interaction. Fig. 4 exhibited the image of SA and SA@PEI-CDs under sunlight and UV-light, respectively. The diameter of SA and SA@PEI-CDs is about 2 mm. SA is colorless and transparent and non-fluorescent under UV-light. SA@PEI-CDs emits blue fluorescence under UV-light, which showed the same color as PEI-CDs. The fluorescent intensity of SA@PEI-CDs adsorbed Pb2+ will decrease in a certain degree (shown in Fig. 4(E)). When SA@PEI-CDs adsorbs Pb2+ to a saturated state, the adsorbent no longer emits fluorescence (shown in Fig. 4(F)). Therefore, the blue fluorescence from the PEI-CDs can indicate the progress of the adsorption process, thereby realizing the visualization of adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The absorption performance of SA@PEI-CDs for Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH value of the solution on the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs is shown in Fig. 5(A). 10 mg SA@PEI-CDs was immersed in the 10 mL 600 mg/L Pb2+ standard solution. According to the solubility product constant (Ksp,Pb(OH)2 = 1.43 × 10−20), Pb(OH)2 will precipitate when pH = 5.34, so we control the pH range of the solution from 1 to 5. When pH changed from 1 to 4 the adsorption capacity increased from 163.56 mg/g to 380.39 mg/g, but when pH further increased to 5, the adsorption capacity slowly decreased to 265.11 mg/g. Under the high concentration of H+, the -NH2 on the surface of SA@PEI-CDs is protonated, which will cause electrostatic repulsion to Pb2+. When pH = 4, -COOH ionizes and is negatively charged, which is beneficial to both the electrostatic adsorption with Pb2+ and the coordination with Pb2+, so the adsorption capacity reaches the maximum.

The quantity of adsorbents also has a direct effect on the adsorption capacity. 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg, 20 mg and 30 mg SA@PEI-CDs were added in Pb2+ standard solution (pH=4, 600 mg/L, 10 mL), respectively. As shown in Fig. 5(B), the removal efficiency increased with the quantity of adsorbents continuously (red line). But the curve of adsorption capacity first rose and then slowly declined (black line). Because with the addition of adsorbents, the numbers of active sites for adsorption increased, the removal efficiency has been increasing. However, in consideration of the adsorption capacity per unit mass, when the amount of SA@PEI-CDs is too much, the adsorption capacity decreased. Therefore, 10 mg is selected as the optimal adsorption amount. At this time, the adsorption capacity is 380.39 mg/g and the removal efficiency is 63.40%. In practical applications, as long as the amount of SA@PEI-CDs is increased, higher removal efficiency can be obtained.

The prepared SA@PEI-CDs hydrogel was added in different initial concentrations Pb2+ standard solution. Both the adsorption capacity of SA and SA@PEI-CDs showed the same trend in Fig. 5(C). With the increase of the initial Pb2+ concentration, the curves first lifted and then maintained equilibrium. Because at low concentrations, the adsorption sites of the adsorbents are not completely occupied. When the initial concentration reached 400 mg/L, the adsorption sites were gradually occupied and formed saturation mode. The adsorption performance was no longer affected by the initial concentration. The maximum adsorption capacity of SA@PEI-CDs is 380.39 mg/g, which enhanced 83.82% compared to SA (206.93 mg/g). The introduction of PEI-CDs provided more adsorption sites for Pb2+.

The adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs at different adsorption time were investigated and shown in Fig. 5(D). With increase of time, the adsorption capacity of SA@PEI-CDs on Pb2+ increased first and reached adsorption equilibrium in 360 min. The equilibrium adsorption capacity was 380.39 mg/g. During the adsorption process, when SA@PEI-CDs was initially added to the Pb2+ solution, the -NH2, -OH, and -COOH on the surface began to chelate or coordinate with Pb2+. Pb2+ can enter SA@PEI-CDs and combine with the internal active adsorption sites until they were saturated. Subsequently, the adsorption capacity will not increase anymore.

Under the condition that the quantity of the adsorbents remains unchanged (10 mg), but the same initial concentration (600 mg/L) coexisted ions are added, the competitive adsorption characteristics of the adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs for Pb2+ are investigated. As shown in Fig. 6(A), when Pb2+ coexisted with Ag+ or Cr(Ⅵ), the removal efficiency for Pb2+ almost remained the same. When Pb2+ coexisted with Fe3+, the removal efficiency was significantly reduced. The reason is that Fe3+ itself has a strong interaction with PEI-CDs. Therefore, Fe3+ caused a side effect on the adsorption of Pb2+. Although the coexisting ions Zn2+, Cu2+, Cr3+ and Ni2+ have a certain effect on the removal rate of Pb2+, Pb2+ are preferentially adsorbed and maintain a high removal rate.

The regeneration and reuse performance of SA@PEI-CDs were studied. We try to use different acids to elute Pb2+ through ion exchange, use EDTA-2Na to complex with Pb2+, and use alkali to generate Pb(OH)2 precipitation to achieve the regeneration of the adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6(B), after the elution by EDTA-2Na, SA@PEI-CDs can keep about 96.72% of the original adsorption capacity, which maintains the maximum adsorption capacity among the eluents. So, EDTA-2Na is an optimal regeneration reagent. After five cycles (Fig. 6(C)), SA@PEI-CDs can still maintain 85.7% of the original adsorption capacity.

Table 1 lists the performance of several adsorbents to remove Pb2+ that have been reported in the literature. Compared with different material systems, the obtained adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs maintains the largest adsorption capacity and exhibits excellent reuse performance at the same time. Its performance is also much higher than that of several hydrogel adsorbents.

Usually, there are two models to describe the adsorption. They are Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models. Langmuir adsorption isotherm model is used to describe monolayer adsorption. It is based on the fact that all adsorption sites have the same energy and adsorption occurs on homogeneous surface of the adsorbent. Freundlich adsorption isotherm model is mainly suitable for heterogeneous surface adsorption sites with different affinities.

For Langmuir isotherm models:
(3)
C
e
Q
e
=
1
K
l
Q
max
+
C
e
Q
max

For Freundlich isotherm models:
(4)
lg
Q
e
= lg
K
f
+
lg C
e
n
where Ce (mg/L) represents for Pb2+ concentration. Qe (mg/g) and Qmax (mg/g) are adsorption capacity at equilibrium time and maximum theoretical adsorption capacity, respectively. Kl (L/mg) and Kf (L/mg) are Langmuir and Freundlich empirical constants and n is concentration index, which are associated with the ability of adsorption.

The adsorption data of SA@PEI-CDs on Pb2+ (listed in Fig. 5(C)) are put into formulas (3) and (4), and K
l (0.02502), Qmax (645.16 mg/g) and K
f (22.21 mg/g), 1/n (0.7286) are calculated respectively.

The dimensionless factor Rl of Langmuir model is defined to predict whether the adsorption process is favorable or unfavorable.
(5)
R
l
=
1
1 +
K
l
C
0

R
l (0.09084) is between 0 and 1, indicating and the adsorption process is effective. 1/n in Freundlich model is the adsorption strength and is related to the specificity of the adsorbate. When 0.1 <1/n < 1, it is beneficial to the adsorption process. At the same time, K
f is large, demonstrating that SA@PEI-CDs has a strong affinity for Pb2+. Taking Ce/Qe as the ordinate and Ce as the abscissa, the graph is shown in Fig. 7(A). With lgQe as the ordinate and lgCe as the abscissa, the graph is shown in Fig. 7(B). The squares of the linear correlation coefficients (R2) of the two straight lines are 0.9863 and 0.9951 respectively. Obviously, the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs is more in line with the Freundlich isotherm model. In addition, the maximum adsorption capacity (645.16 mg/g) calculated according to the Langmuir model formula (3) is very different from the actual measured value of 380.39 mg/g, which also shows that the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs does not follow this model.

There are two classic kinetics models to evaluate the adsorption kinetic process. They are pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO). PFO is based on Lagergern model. The model assumes that there is no chemical interaction between the adsorbent and the adsorbate, and adsorption is a physical process. PSO is mainly based on the chemical adsorption process through covalent bonds and ion exchange, which involves electron sharing or electron transfer between the adsorbent and the adsorbate.

For PFO model:
(6)
ln
Q
e
−
Q
t
= ln
Q
e
−
K
1
t

For PSO model:
(7)
t
Q
t
=
1
K
2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q
e
K1(/min) and K2(g/mg/min) are rate constants of PFO and PSO models, respectively.

According to the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs at different time (shown in Fig. 5 (D)), K1 and K2 are calculated as 0.0132 and 6.9 × 10−5 respectively. Plot ln (Qe-Qt) versus t (Fig. 7(C)) gets the square of the linear correlation coefficient as 0.9228. Plot t/Qt versus t (Fig. 7 (D)) gets the square of the linear correlation coefficient as 0.9991. Therefore, the adsorption kinetic process of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs is more fitted to PSO model.

In order to study the spontaneity and energy changes in the adsorption process, ∆G (free energy change), ∆H (enthalpy) and ∆S (entropy) were calculated according to the thermodynamic Eqs. (8) and (9) by changing the reaction temperature.
(8)
∆ G = − RT ln
Qe
Ce
(9)
∆ G = ∆ H − T ∆ S
where T (K) is the Kelvin temperature and R (8.314 J•mol−1K−1) is the gas constant.

When 10 mg SA@PEI-CDs were added to 600 mg/L Pb2+ standard solution (pH=4) and the temperature was changed from 298.15 K to 338.15 K, the ∆G, ∆H and ∆S are calculated and presented in Table 2. It can be seen that the ∆G < 0 under above conditions which indicates the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs is a spontaneous process. As the temperature increases, the absolute value of ∆G gradually increases, suggesting that heating is beneficial to the reaction. According to the formula, ∆H= +7.763 kJ/mol and ∆S= +29.59 J/mol•K were calculated. It means the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs is endothermic and entropy increasing reaction.

The surface morphology changes of SA@PEI-CDs before and after Pb2+ adsorption is shown in Fig. S5. It can be seen that the surface of SA@PEI-CDs is rough and there are some wrinkles compared to the smooth surface of SA hydrogel beads. When Pb2+ is adsorbed, many vesicle-like protrusions appeared on the surface of SA@PEI-CDs. This may be caused by the entry of Pb2+ into the hydrogel, or by the exchange of Ca2+ and Pb2+ during the adsorption process. Because the ionic radius of Pb2+ is significantly larger than that of Ca2+, Pb2+ replaces the Ca2+ in SA@PEI-CDs, which will inevitably lead to the change surface morphology of the hydrogel.

Fig. 8(A) is thermogravimetry analysis curves of SA, SA@PEI-CDs and SA@PEI-CDs adsorbed Pb2+. The thermogravimetric law of SA and SA@PEI-CDs is basically the same, which is mainly divided into three stages. First, from 20 °C to 200 °C, a weight loss of about 17% occurs. This is caused by the loss of physically adsorbed H2O and hydrogen bonded H2O. Then, from 200 °C to 310 °C, the sodium alginate molecular skeleton undergoes thermal decomposition, followed by the gentle decomposition of functional groups, until the weight does not change. When the temperature is greater than 500 °C, the decomposition of PEI-CDs is accompanied by the decomposition of sodium alginate functional groups until the temperature rises to 800 °C. After the adsorption of Pb2+, the pyrolysis temperature changes significantly. First, the evaporation temperature of H2O is 145 °C, and the weight loss is only 8%. This is because part of Pb2+ taking place of H2O combined with SA@PEI-CDs via physical interaction. Secondly, from 171 °C to 330 °C, the sodium alginate skeleton is thermally decomposed. The pyrolysis temperature of functional groups and the decomposition of PEI-CDs are shortened to 530 °C. The reason is that most of the functional groups in the hydrogel are combined with Pb2+, which ultimately leads to a decrease in the degradation of functional groups and a relatively increase in the degradation of the sodium alginate skeleton.

Fig. 8(B) and Fig. 8(C) show the FT-IR spectrum and UV–vis spectrum of SA, SA@PEI-CDs and SA@PEI-CDs adsorbed Pb2+. Due to the less doped PEI-CDs, the FT-IR spectrum of SA@PEI-CDs is similar to that of SA, which mainly reflects the functional group characteristics of SA. When SA@PEI-CDs adsorbed Pb2+ ions, the position of the association peak derived from -OH shifted slightly. However, the C-O vibration or O-H in-plane bending vibration peak derived from carboxylic acid or carboxylate located near 1400 cm−1 has a significant blue shift and its intensity has increased. This may be caused by the coordination of Pb2+ and carboxyl groups. UV–vis absorption spectrum reveals when SA combines with PEI-CDs, the absorption peak (318 nm) of the π-π transition derived from PEI-CDs is red-shifted, and the conjugation of -COO- in SA with the graphene-like plane of PEI-CDs is enhanced so that a platform absorption expanding to 350 nm in SA@PEI-CDs appears. When SA@PEI-CDs adsorbs Pb2+ ions, the platform absorption has a blue shift, which is the weakening of the conjugation effect caused by the coordination of carboxyl groups with Pb2+.

To further study the adsorption mechanism of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs, the intra-particle diffusion model was used to fit the kinetic data. The formula of the intra-particle diffusion model is:
(10)
Q
t
=
k
id
×
t
1 / 2
+ C
where k
id is the internal diffusion rate constant（mg/g˙min−0.5）, C is the constant that reflects the boundary layer effect, t is time (min). As shown in Fig. 8(D), the kinetic data (from Fig. 5(D)) can be fitted into three-stage linear relationship. Due to the line dose not pass through the origin, it indicates that intraparticle diffusion is not the only limited step of the adsorption rate. In the first stage, the slope of the line is larger, indicating that the diffusion resistance is smaller, which corresponds to the diffusion of the boundary layer, that is, Pb2+ migrates from the main liquid phase to the surface of SA@PEI-CDs. The decrease in the slope of the second stage line indicates that the diffusion resistance increases and the diffusion speed decreases. It corresponds to the diffusion of Pb2+ into the inner surface of SA@PEI-CDs after surface adsorption is saturated. The third stage has the lowest slope. At this point the adsorption has reached equilibrium.

In order to understand the adsorption mechanism in depth, the XPS spectra before and after the adsorption of Pb2+ by SA@PEI-CDs were measured and compared with that of SA. As shown in Fig. 9(A), for the element composition, SA mainly contains C, O and Ca, SA@PEI-CDs also contains N. After Pb2+ is adsorbed, the peak of Ca element disappears, while that of Pb element is clearly visible. Fig. 9(B) further confirms that the binding energy of Pb 4f5/2 and Pb 4f7/2 are 143.5 eV and 138.6 eV, respectively. This is quite different from the bonding energies (139.2 eV and 144.1 eV) of the two Pb 4f peaks in Pb(NO3)2 [42]. It indicates that there is an interaction between Pb2+ and the functional groups on SA@PEI-CD during the adsorption process. Fig. 9(C) shows the peaks belonging to Ca 2P1/2 (352.2 eV) and Ca 2P3/2 (347.6 eV) derived from SA almost disappear after Pb2+ ions are adsorbed in SA@PEI-CDs, which proves that there existed ion exchange between Ca2+ and Pb2+ in the adsorption process. It can be seen from the C1s spectrum in Fig. 9(D) that the peaks with binding energies of 284.56 eV, 286.08 eV and 288.22 eV correspond to CC, CO/CN and -CO/-COOH in SA@PEI-CDs, respectively. The bonding energy is basically unchanged after adsorption of Pb2+. However, in the spectra of N1s and O1s, it can be found that after Pb2+ is adsorbed, the binding energy of functional groups have changed significantly. The binding energy of N-H and C-N changed from 399.28 eV and 400.15–399.69 eV and 400.54 eV, respectively. The binding energy of -CO moved from 531.95 eV to 532.24 eV, while that of C-OH moved from 532.99 eV to 533.08 eV. This shows that all these functional groups can coordinate with Pb2+.

The above characterizations show that there are several different mechanisms that simultaneously exist in the adsorption process of SA@PEI-CDs on Pb2+.
(1)
Ion exchange: exchange of Ca2+ and Pb2+. It has been confirmed by SEM and XPS analysis. In addition, some Ca2+ (137.69 mg/L) are detected in the solution after the adsorption of Pb2+.
(2)
Surface complexation: coordination of Pb2+ with the functional groups of SA@PEI-CDs. It has been verified by pseudo-second-order (PSO) kinetics model and XPS, FT-IR analysis.
(3)
Electrostatic adsorption: interaction between Pb2+ and negatively charged groups on the surface of adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs especially under weak acidic conditions. The Zeta potential of SA is − 2.7 mV, and it remains negative after SA interacts with PEI-CDs, which is beneficial to the electrostatic adsorption between SA@PEI-CDs and Pb2+.
(4)
Intraparticle diffusion: Pb2+ migrates from the main liquid phase to the surface of SA@PEI-CDs. It is verified via intra-particle diffusion model.

As shown in Fig. S6, in the initial stage of adsorption, the main mechanisms are intra-particle diffusion and electrostatic adsorption. Then chemical adsorption including surface coordination and ion exchange mechanism dominate the adsorption process. The combination of PEI-CDs and SA increases the nitrogen-containing groups, which greatly enhances the surface chelation of the adsorbent and Pb2+, thereby increasing the adsorption efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of simulated samples of surface water and drinking water
ce:section.ce:para: According to the Surface Water Environmental Quality Standard (GB3838–2002) a mixed solution of heavy metal ions was prepared. The concentration of Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr(Ⅵ) and Pb2+ are 1.0, 5.0, 0.1, 1.0, 1.0 mg/L, respectively, that are 100 times higher than the standard requires. The adsorption experiment was carried out in the designed three-stage water purifier mode (presented in Fig. S7), the quantity of adsorbent per stage was 50 mg, and the total adsorption time was 9 h. ICP-MS was used to detect the concentration of the remaining metal ions in the solution after each level of adsorption, and the results are listed in Table. S1.

For surface water, the results show that after the second adsorption purification, the concentration of Zn2+ and Pb2+ reduced to 0.04814 and 0.002540 mg/L (less than the 0.05 mg/L and 0.01 mg/L required by the quality standard). It means that the water has met the primary surface water environmental quality standards. But for Cd2+, Cr(Ⅵ) and Cu2+, another level adsorption and purification are needed. For drinking water, according to the Drinking Water Hygiene Standard (GB5749–2006) a sample contains Cr(Ⅵ) (5 mg/L), Cd2+ (0.5 mg/L) and Pb2+ (1 mg/L) was prepared. The concentration is 100 times higher than the standard requires. After three times adsorption purification, the concentration of Cd2+, Cr(Ⅵ) and Pb2+ reduced to 0.009648 mg/L, 0.003868 mg/L and 0.003611 mg/L. (shown in Table. S2). It indicates that the adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs has excellent universal adsorption characteristics for heavy metal ions with the characteristics of easy recovery and easy regeneration. Therefore, it is expected to be widely used in water purification and other fields.

CDs were successfully synthesized from edible waste broccoli stems. The modification of PEI not only improves the fluorescence characteristics of CDs but also changes the interaction between CDs and Pb2+ ions. The spherical adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs formed by the electrostatic self-assembly of SA and PEI-CDs can efficiently adsorb Pb2+ ions with adsorption capacity of 380.39 mg/g, which increased by 83.82% comparing to that of bare SA. The fluorescence emission characteristics of PEI-CDs make the adsorption process of Pb2+ visible. SA@PEI-CDs is also easy to regenerate and extremely convenient to recycle and reuse. The results show that the adsorption data fit the Freundlich isotherm model and follow the pseudo -second-order kinetic model. Adsorption is an exothermic process with increasing entropy. The adsorption mechanism of SA@PEI-CDs on Pb2+ ions is mainly chemical adsorption, including surface complexation and cation exchange, supplemented by electrostatic attraction and intra-particle diffusion. The adsorbent SA@PEI-CDs is applied to our own multi-layer water purifier. The simulated heavy metal polluted water sample can meet the first-level water quality standard after being adsorbed by three layers, in which the concentration of Pb2+ in surface water and drinking water is decreased from 1.0 mg/L to 0.002031 mg/L and 0.003611 mg/L, respectively. Therefore, the adsorbent is expected to play a certain role in the treatment of heavy metal polluted wastewater and environmental protection.

Siyi Zhang: Synthesis, Characterization, Design, Application in Pb2+ adsorption, Mechanism analysis. Kamile Arkin: Data analysis. Yuxin Zheng: Synthesis and characterization. Jicheng Ma: ICP-AES and ICP-MS test. Yuyang Bei: Synthesis. Di Liu: Mechanism analysis. Qingkun Shang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22172021, 21872022, 21573039).

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material

.
